rules.description: Em-dash abuse. Delete all. Use periods, commas, or restructure.

bad: I built it — and it worked
The tool — which I use daily — is great
good: The tool I use daily is great.

rules.description: En-dash abuse. Same treatment as em-dashes.

bad: Monday – Friday

rules.description: Authority signaling phrases. Delete the setup, state directly.

matcher.pattern: \b(?:[Tt]he uncomfortable truth|[Ll]et me be clear|[Hh]ere'?s what nobody tells you|[Tt]he hard truth is|[Hh]ere'?s the reality|[Ww]hat most people miss(?:\s+is)?)\b

bad: The uncomfortable truth about GTM is...
Let me be clear: this works.

rules.description: Narrator setup lines. Delete the setup, start with the actual point.

matcher.pattern: \b(?:[Hh]ere'?s the thing about|[Hh]ere'?s where it gets interesting|[Hh]ere'?s the real (?:value|story|reason)|[Hh]ere'?s the actual |[Tt]he real (?:story|question|issue) is)\b

bad: Here's the thing about webhook pipelines...
Here's where it gets interesting.

rules.description: Dramatic rhetorical framing. State what happened. Let the reader feel it.

matcher.pattern: \b(?:[Bb]ut here'?s the part where|[Aa]nd that'?s when it clicked|[Ww]ant to know the crazy part)\b

bad: But here's the part where it gets real.
And that's when it clicked.

rules.description: Three parallel dramatic sentences. One direct statement lands harder. Cut to one.

matcher.note: Three short parallel sentences with similar structure. Hard to regex reliably. Flagged by manual review or LLM-based detection.

bad: You can't see it. You can't copy-paste it away. You have to know it exists.
good: You have to know it exists.

rules.description: Opening thesis restated at the end. Real thinking goes somewhere new by the end.

bad: In summary, the pipeline works.
In conclusion, we shipped it.
good: We shipped it. Here's what broke.

rules.description: Self-branded concepts. Just explain it.

matcher.pattern: \b(?:[Tt]his is what [Ii] call|[Ii] like to call this|[Ww]hat [Ii] refer to as)\b

bad: This is what I call the 'signal stack'.
good: The signal stack pulls from three sources.

rules.description: Artificial drama sentences. Show why it's hard with a specific example.

matcher.pattern: [Tt]he shift sounds simple\.\s*[Ii]t'?s not\.|[Ss]ounds easy\.\s*[Ii]t isn'?t\.

bad: The shift sounds simple. It's not.
good: The shift took 3 weeks because the schema migration broke twice.

rules.description: Colon-listed everything. Reads like a PowerPoint slide. Write natural sentences.

bad: the result: better data. the impact: faster sales.

rules.description: Humble brag disclaimer. Share your take or don't. The disclaimer makes it worse.

matcher.pattern: \b(?:[Ii] don'?t have all the answers,? but|[Ii]'?m no expert,? but|[Nn]ot to brag,? but)\b

bad: I don't have all the answers, but here's what worked.

rules.description: Lazy energy words that say nothing. Replace with what actually changed or happened.

bad: This tool is a game changer.
Unleash your pipeline.
good: This tool cut our enrichment time from 4 hours to 20 minutes.

rules.description: Saying 'no fluff' is itself fluff. If the content has no fluff, the reader will know.

bad: No fluff, just tactics.
No BS guide to cron jobs.

rules.description: Performative emphasis that reads as AI padding. Cut it.

bad: No templates, nada.
Nada. Just vibes.

rules.description: Sycophantic openers that signal AI-generated responses.

bad: Great question! Here's how...
Absolutely! The way I see it...

rules.description: Opens with YOU instead of VALUE. The reader doesn't care about your process.

bad: Dropping this resource for anyone who needs it.
Sharing something I built last week.
good: Resource: how to automate deploys with GitHub Actions.
I built a scoring model last week. It does X.

rules.description: Announcement-style title with no value proposition.

bad: My new course is live.
good: My new course teaches you to build CI pipelines from scratch in 2 hours.

rules.description: Ellipsis used to build false suspense before a common word.

bad: I tried everything... and then it worked.
good: I tried everything. Then it worked.

rules.description: Overused transition phrases that signal AI-generated structure.

bad: That said, the tool works.
That being said, I'd still use it.

rules.description: Filler transition phrases that add nothing.

bad: Without further ado, here's the workflow.

rules.description: Engagement bait endings. Generally avoid, but OK on meme posts.

matcher.pattern: \b(?:[Ss]o here'?s my question for you|[Ww]hat do you think\?|[Cc]urious to hear your thoughts|[Dd]rop a comment)\b

bad: So here's my question for you: what's your stack?
good: What's your stack? Drop it in the comments.

rules.context_note: Allow on meme/social posts where audience participation is the point.

rules.description: Arguments belong in prose. Bullets for execution, prose for reasoning.

matcher.note: Bulleted list used to present reasoning or arguments instead of prose. Bullets are fine for steps, tools, and specs.

bad:
Why this matters:
- It saves time
- It reduces errors
- It scales better
good: This saves time because the enrichment runs in parallel. Fewer manual steps means fewer errors. And it scales to 10K rows without changes.

rules.description: False dichotomies. Avoid, but legitimate old-vs-new contrasts are fine.

bad: It's not luck, it's strategy.

rules.context_note: Allow when contrasting old way vs. new way (manual SDR grind vs. automated workflow).

rules.description: Headers for navigation only. LinkedIn formatting is whitespace-driven.

bad: **The Problem** Most GTM teams...

rules.description: Quoted phrases that don't need quotes. Write them as plain text.

bad: I call this a "growth loop".
good: I call this a growth loop.

rules.context_note: Allow for actual quotations from other people or named concepts.

rules.description: Ellipses for trailing thoughts. Part of natural voice.
Arrows for workflow steps and progression. Part of natural voice.
Emoji section markers for structuring workflow walkthroughs.
Directional 'here's how/the play' openers. Part of natural voice, not narrator setup.

good: Here's how I set up the scoring pipeline.
